"text_saheeh_international": "The foolish among the people will say, \"What has turned them away from their qiblah, which they used to face?\" Say, \"To Allah belongs the east and the west. He guides whom He wills to a straight path.\"",

"arabic_text": "۞ سَيَقُولُ ٱلسُّفَهَآءُ مِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَا وَلَّىٰهُمْ عَن قِبْلَتِهِمُ ٱلَّتِى كَانُوا۟ عَلَيْهَا ۚ قُل لِّلَّهِ ٱلْمَشْرِقُ وَٱلْمَغْرِبُ ۚ يَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ"

"text_saheeh_international": "Thus We have made you a moderate community that you may be witnesses over mankind and the Messenger may be a witness over you. And We did not make the qiblah which you used to face except that We might make evident who would follow the Messenger from who would turn back on his heels. And indeed, it was difficult except for those whom Allah has guided. And never would Allah have caused you to lose your faith. Indeed Allah is, to the people, Kind and Merciful.",

"arabic_text": "وَكَذَٰلِكَ جَعَلْنَٰكُمْ أُمَّةً وَسَطًا لِّتَكُونُوا۟ شُهَدَآءَ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ وَيَكُونَ ٱلرَّسُولُ عَلَيْكُمْ شَهِيدًا ۗ وَمَا جَعَلْنَا ٱلْقِبْلَةَ ٱلَّتِى كُنتَ عَلَيْهَآ إِلَّآ لِنَعْلَمَ مَن يَتَّبِعُ ٱلرَّسُولَ مِمَّن يَنقَلِبُ عَلَىٰ عَقِبَيْهِ ۚ وَإِن كَانَتْ لَكَبِيرَةً إِلَّا عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ هَدَى ٱللَّهُ ۗ وَمَا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ لِيُضِيعَ إِيمَٰنَكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِٱلنَّاسِ لَرَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ"

"text_saheeh_international": "We have certainly seen the turning of your face, [O Muhammad], toward the heaven, and We will surely turn you to a qiblah with which you will be pleased. So turn your face toward al-Masjid al-Haram. And wherever you [believers] are, turn your faces toward it [in prayer]. Indeed, those who have been given the Scripture know that it is the truth from their Lord. And Allah is not unaware of what they do.",

"arabic_text": "قَدْ نَرَىٰ تَقَلُّبَ وَجْهِكَ فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ ۖ فَلَنُوَلِّيَنَّكَ قِبْلَةً تَرْضَىٰهَا ۚ فَوَلِّ وَجْهَكَ شَطْرَ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ ۚ وَحَيْثُ مَا كُنتُمْ فَوَلُّوا۟ وُجُوهَكُمْ شَطْرَهُۥ ۗ وَإِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَٰبَ لَيَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّهُ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّهِمْ ۗ وَمَا ٱللَّهُ بِغَٰفِلٍ عَمَّا يَعْمَلُونَ"

"text_saheeh_international": "And even if you brought to those who were given the Scripture every sign, they would not follow your qiblah. Nor are you to follow their qiblah. Nor do they follow one another's qiblah. And if you were to follow their desires after what has come to you of knowledge, you would then be among the wrongdoers.",

"arabic_text": "وَلَئِنْ أَتَيْتَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَٰبَ بِكُلِّ ءَايَةٍ مَّا تَبِعُوا۟ قِبْلَتَكَ ۚ وَمَآ أَنتَ بِتَابِعٍ قِبْلَتَهُمْ ۚ وَمَا بَعْضُهُم بِتَابِعٍ قِبْلَةَ بَعْضٍ ۚ وَلَئِنِ ٱتَّبَعْتَ أَهْوَآءَهُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَكَ مِنَ ٱلْعِلْمِ ۙ إِنَّكَ إِذًا لَّمِنَ ٱلظَّٰلِمِينَ"
"text_saheeh_international": "Those to whom We gave the Scripture recognize him as they recognize their [own] sons. But indeed, a party of them conceal the truth while they know it.",
"arabic_text": "ٱلَّذِينَ ءَاتَيْنَٰهُمُ ٱلْكِتَٰبَ يَعْرِفُونَهُۥ كَمَا يَعْرِفُونَ أَبْنَآءَهُمْ ۖ وَإِنَّ فَرِيقًا مِّنْهُمْ لَيَكْتُمُونَ ٱلْحَقَّ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ"
"text_saheeh_international": "The truth is from your Lord, so never be among the doubters.",
"arabic_text": "ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكَ فَلَا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْمُمْتَرِينَ"

"text_saheeh_international": "For every nation is a direction to which it turns. So race to all that is good. Wherever you may be, Allah will bring you forth [for judgement] all together. Indeed, Allah is over all things competent.",
"arabic_text": "وَلِكُلٍّ وِجْهَةٌ هُوَ مُوَلِّيهَا ۖ فَٱسْتَبِقُوا۟ ٱلْخَيْرَٰتِ ۚ أَيْنَ مَا تَكُونُوا۟ يَأْتِ بِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ جَمِيعًا ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ"

"text_saheeh_international": "And from wherever you go out [for prayer], turn your face toward al-Masjid al-Haram. And indeed, it is the truth from your Lord. And Allah is not unaware of what you do.",
"arabic_text": "وَمِنْ حَيْثُ خَرَجْتَ فَوَلِّ وَجْهَكَ شَطْرَ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ ۖ وَإِنَّهُۥ لَلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكَ ۗ وَمَا ٱللَّهُ بِغَٰفِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ"

"text_saheeh_international": "And from wherever you go out [for prayer], turn your face toward al-Masjid al-Haram. And wherever you [believers] are, turn your faces toward it [in prayer], so that people will not have argument against you, except for those of them who commit wrong. So fear them not, but fear Me. And [thus] that I may complete My favor upon you and that you may be guided.",

"arabic_text": "وَمِنْ حَيْثُ خَرَجْتَ فَوَلِّ وَجْهَكَ شَطْرَ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ ۚ وَحَيْثُ مَا كُنتُمْ فَوَلُّوا۟ وُجُوهَكُمْ شَطْرَهُۥ لِئَلَّا يَكُونَ لِلنَّاسِ عَلَيْكُمْ حُجَّةٌ إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ فَلَا تَخْشَوْهُمْ وَٱخْشَوْنِى وَلِأُتِمَّ نِعْمَتِى عَلَيْكُمْ وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ"

"text_saheeh_international": "Just as We have sent among you a messenger from yourselves reciting to you Our verses and purifying you and teaching you the Book and wisdom and teaching you that which you did not know.",
"arabic_text": "كَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا فِيكُمْ رَسُولًا مِّنكُمْ يَتْلُوا۟ عَلَيْكُمْ ءَايَٰتِنَا وَيُزَكِّيكُمْ وَيُعَلِّمُكُمُ ٱلْكِتَٰبَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ وَيُعَلِّمُكُم مَّا لَمْ تَكُونُوا۟ تَعْلَمُونَ"
"text_saheeh_international": "So remember Me; I will remember you. And be grateful to Me and do not deny Me.",

"explanation": "**Context of the Qiblah Change (2:142)**
This verse provides a divine pre-emption, warning the believers about the criticisms they would face from "the foolish among the people" regarding the change of the Qiblah from Bayt al-Maqdis (Jerusalem) to the Ka'bah. Al-Qurtubi clarifies that "fools" (سُفَهَاء) here refers to the ignorant wrongdoers, specifically identifying them as the Jews, hypocrites, and unbelievers of Quraysh, who would mock the Muslims for this shift in prayer direction. The change occurred after the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) had prayed towards Jerusalem for a period, with scholarly disagreement on the exact duration (e.g., 16-17 months) and the specific prayer (Asr, Subh, or Dhuhr) during which the change was first implemented, leading to the mosque being named "Masjid Dhul-Qiblatayn" (Mosque of the Two Qiblahs).

**Theological Implications of Abrogation and Divine Sovereignty (2:142)**
Al-Qurtubi highlights that this verse serves as clear evidence for the principle of abrogation (naskh) in Islamic law, a concept largely agreed upon by the Muslim community, with rare exceptions. He notes that the Qiblah was the first ruling to be abrogated in the Qur'an, and potentially abrogated twice according to some views. Furthermore, it demonstrates the permissibility of the Sunnah being abrogated by the Qur'an, as the initial direction towards Jerusalem was based on the Prophet's practice (Sunnah) rather than an explicit Qur'anic text, which was then superseded by a Qur'anic command. The verse "To Allah belongs the east and the west" underscores Allah's absolute sovereignty and right to command any direction for worship, as all directions are His creation and property. His guidance to a "straight path" (صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ) signifies His wisdom in directing this Ummah to the Qiblah of Ibrahim.

**The Status of the Ummah and the Test of Obedience (2:143)**
Al-Qurtubi explains that Allah's designation of the Muslim community as an "Ummah Wasat" (a middlemost, just, and balanced community) signifies their elevated status, being inferior to Prophets but superior to other nations. This balance implies avoiding extremes and upholding justice. This status also entails their role as witnesses against mankind on the Day of Resurrection, testifying to the conveyance of divine messages by previous prophets, as exemplified by the hadith concerning Prophet Nuh. The change of Qiblah itself was a divine test, designed to distinguish those who truly followed the Messenger from those who would waver or apostatize. The phrase "We only appointed the direction you used to face in order to know" is interpreted as Allah's desire to manifest this knowledge, distinguishing certainty from doubt among His servants. The difficulty of this change was significant, except for those whose hearts Allah had guided, reinforcing the idea that true faith manifests in unwavering obedience to divine commands, even when challenging.

**Preservation of Previous Prayers and Divine Mercy (2:143)**
Al-Qurtubi emphasizes that Allah's statement, "Allah would never let your faith go to waste," was revealed to reassure Muslims who had died praying towards Jerusalem before the Qiblah change, confirming the validity and reward for their prayers. This interpretation refutes the Murji'ite theological position by affirming that prayer is an integral part of faith (Iman). The verse concludes by highlighting Allah's profound gentleness (Ra'fah) and mercy (Rahmah) towards mankind, with Ra'fah being described as a stronger form of compassion than Rahmah, indicating Allah's immense care for His servants and His preservation of their righteous deeds.

**The Prophet's Longing and the Ka'bah as Qiblah (2:144)**
This verse addresses the Prophet's yearning for the Ka'bah as the Qiblah, indicated by his "turning of your face towards the heaven" in anticipation of divine revelation. Al-Qurtubi clarifies that the command "turn your face toward al-Masjid al-Haram" means facing the Ka'bah itself for those who can see it, and its general direction for those who cannot. He notes that for those within Masjid al-Haram, facing any part of the Ka'bah is sufficient, while for those in Makkah, facing the mosque is sufficient, and for the rest of the Ummah, facing the direction of Makkah. The verse also asserts that the People of the Book (Jews and Christians) were aware of this impending change from their scriptures, recognizing it as "the truth from their Lord," yet many concealed this knowledge due to envy and obstinacy. This highlights their deliberate rejection of truth despite clear evidence.

**The Stubbornness of the People of the Scripture (2:145-147)**
Al-Qurtubi elaborates on the profound obstinacy of the People of the Scripture, stating that even if the Prophet presented "every sign," they would not follow his Qiblah. This is attributed to their deep-seated disbelief and adherence to their own desires. The verse also underscores the distinct Qiblahs of the Jews and Christians, noting their mutual disagreement, which further emphasizes their deviation from a unified truth. The warning "if you were to follow their desires... you would then be among the wrongdoers" is primarily directed at the Muslim community, emphasizing the gravity of deviating from divine guidance, even though addressed to the Prophet for heightened emphasis. Verses 146 and 147 reiterate that the People of the Scripture "recognize him [Muhammad or the truth of the Qiblah change] as they recognize their [own] sons," a powerful analogy for absolute certainty, yet they "conceal the truth while they know it." This deliberate concealment, despite undeniable knowledge, is presented as a grave transgression, with the final affirmation that "The truth is from your Lord, so never be among the doubters," reinforcing the divine origin and certainty of the Islamic message.

**Racing to Good Deeds and Divine Omnipotence (2:148)**
Al-Qurtubi interprets "For every nation is a direction to which it turns" as referring to the Qiblah, whether divinely appointed or chosen by whims. The subsequent command, "So race to all that is good," is an exhortation to hasten to all acts of obedience, with a particular emphasis on performing prayers at the beginning of their prescribed times. He discusses the scholarly debate regarding the optimal timing for various prayers, citing Malik's nuanced view on delaying Dhuhr in intense heat or 'Isha for congregational prayer, while preferring early performance for Subh and Maghrib. The verse concludes by affirming Allah's omnipotence, stating, "Wherever you may be, Allah will bring you forth [for judgement] all together. Indeed, Allah is over all things competent," emphasizing His ability to resurrect and gather all humanity for accountability, regardless of their location or state of decay.

**Reinforcement of the Qiblah Command and its Wisdom (2:149-150)**
Al-Qurtubi explains the repetition of the command to face the Ka'bah (verses 149 and 150) as a means of emphasizing its immense importance and to solidify its acceptance among the believers, especially given the difficulty of the change. This repetition also serves to counter any lingering arguments from opponents. The phrase "so that people will not have argument against you" refers to the removal of any legitimate basis for criticism from the Arab idolaters, who revered the Ka'bah. The exception, "except for those of them who commit wrong," indicates that the arguments of the obstinate and unjust are baseless and should not be feared. The command "So fear them not, but fear Me" distinguishes between the fear of human criticism (khawf) and the profound reverence and awe of Allah (khashyah). The ultimate wisdom behind this change is "that I may complete My favor upon you and that you may be guided," signifying the perfection of the Islamic Shari'ah, the guidance to the true Qiblah, and ultimately, admission into Paradise.

**The Blessings of Prophethood and the Call to Remembrance (2:151-152)**
Al-Qurtubi connects verse 151 to the completion of Allah's blessings, specifically through the sending of Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him). This Messenger, chosen from among the people, recites Allah's verses, purifies the believers, teaches them the Book (Qur'an) and wisdom (Sunnah), and imparts knowledge previously unknown to them. This transformative role elevates the community from ignorance to spiritual and intellectual enlightenment. Verse 152 then issues a direct command: "So remember Me; I will remember you. And be grateful to Me and do not deny Me." Al-Qurtubi interprets "remember Me" (فَٱذْكُرُونِىٓ) as remembering Allah through obedience, worship, and gratitude, while "I will remember you" (أَذْكُرْكُمْ) signifies Allah's remembrance of His servant with reward, mercy, and forgiveness. He emphasizes that true remembrance is manifested through obedience, not merely verbal glorification. "Be grateful to Me" (وَٱشْكُرُوا۟ لِى) means acknowledging and displaying Allah's blessings, while "do not deny Me" (وَلَا تَكْفُرُونِ) refers to avoiding ingratitude by covering up or failing to acknowledge these blessings, rather than outright disbelief (kufr). This highlights the profound reciprocal relationship between the Creator and His creation, where human remembrance and gratitude are met with divine favor and remembrance.

"explanation": "**The Context of the Qiblah Change and the 'Fools' (2:142)**
Ibn Kathir elucidates the historical context of this verse, which addresses the anticipated criticisms from "the foolish among the people" (السُّفَهَاءُ مِنَ النَّاسِ) regarding the change of the Qiblah. He identifies these "fools" as the People of the Scripture (Jews and Christians), hypocrites, and idolaters, who would question the Muslims' shift from praying towards Bayt al-Maqdis (Jerusalem) to the Ka'bah in Makkah. The Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) had initially prayed towards Jerusalem for approximately sixteen or seventeen months after his migration to Madinah, while inwardly desiring to face the Ka'bah. This divine instruction to change the Qiblah was a significant event, as narrated in authentic Hadith from Al-Bara bin 'Azib and Ibn 'Umar, where companions in prayer immediately turned towards the Ka'bah upon receiving the news. The verse asserts Allah's absolute dominion over all directions, "To Allah belong both, east and the west," signifying that the command and authority are His alone, and believers are to obey His directives wherever they may lead, as He "guides whom He wills to a straight path."

**The Status of the Ummah and the Test of Faith (2:143)**
Ibn Kathir explains that the change of Qiblah was intrinsically linked to Allah's elevation of the Muslim Ummah to the status of a "Wasat" (just and best) nation. This designation implies that they possess the most complete legislation and the clearest methodology, enabling them to be "witnesses over mankind" on the Day of Resurrection, testifying to the conveyance of divine messages by previous prophets. He cites the Hadith of Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, where Prophet Nuh (peace be upon him) will be asked about conveying his message, and this Ummah will testify on his behalf, with Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) attesting to their testimony. The Qiblah change served as a profound test, distinguishing true followers of the Messenger from those who would "turn back on their heels" due to hypocrisy or weak faith. Allah's assurance, "And Allah would never make your faith (prayers) to be lost," was revealed to affirm the validity and reward for the prayers performed towards Jerusalem by those who had died before the change, underscoring Allah's immense kindness and mercy towards His servants.

**Divine Fulfillment and the Ka'bah's Significance (2:144)**
Ibn Kathir details the Prophet's longing for the Ka'bah, observing his "turning of your face towards the heaven" in anticipation of revelation. Allah's response, "We will surely turn you to a qiblah with which you will be pleased," signifies the divine fulfillment of this desire. The command to face "al-Masjid al-Haram" (the Sacred Mosque) means facing its direction from any location on earth. He clarifies that while the general direction is sufficient, exceptions exist for voluntary prayers during travel or in times of battle, and for those genuinely mistaken about the direction. Crucially, Ibn Kathir emphasizes that the People of the Scripture possessed prior knowledge from their own books about the Prophet's description and the eventual change of Qiblah to the Ka'bah, yet they denied this truth due to envy and disbelief. This highlights their deliberate rejection of clear divine signs.

**The Stubbornness of the People of the Scripture (2:145-147)**
Ibn Kathir underscores the profound disbelief and stubbornness of the Jews, stating that even if the Prophet presented "every sign," they would not abandon their desires to follow his Qiblah. This verse highlights the Prophet's unwavering adherence to Allah's commands, contrasting it with the Jews' obstinacy. It also notes the internal divisions among the People of the Scripture, as "they will not follow each other's Qiblah," further illustrating their deviation from a unified truth. The warning, "if you were to follow their desires after what has come to you of knowledge, you would then be among the wrongdoers," serves as a severe caution against knowingly defying the truth, emphasizing that the accountability for those with knowledge is greater. Verses 146 and 147 reinforce that the People of the Scripture "recognize him [Muhammad or the truth of the Qiblah change] as they recognize their [own] sons," a powerful metaphor for their undeniable certainty. Despite this clear recognition, "a party of them conceal the truth while they know it," demonstrating their deliberate obfuscation. The concluding affirmation, "The truth is from your Lord, so never be among the doubters," strengthens the resolve of the Prophet and the believers against such deliberate falsehoods.

**Divine Omnipotence and the Call to Good Deeds (2:148)**
Ibn Kathir interprets "For every nation is a direction to which it turns" as acknowledging that various religious groups have their own prayer directions, but Allah's chosen Qiblah is for the believers. The subsequent exhortation, "So race to all that is good," encourages believers to hasten towards all forms of righteousness and obedience. This verse also serves as a reminder of Allah's absolute power and ability to resurrect and gather all humanity for judgment, regardless of their physical state or location, as "Wherever you may be, Allah will bring you forth [for judgement] all together. Indeed, Allah is over all things competent." This emphasizes accountability and the ultimate return to Allah.

**The Repetition of the Qiblah Command and its Wisdom (2:149-150)**
Ibn Kathir explains the repetition of the command to face the Ka'bah (verses 149 and 150) as a reinforcement of its importance and to address different aspects of its significance. The first mention (2:144) was a fulfillment of the Prophet's wish. The subsequent repetitions serve to emphasize that this change is indeed "the truth from your Lord" and to remove any "argument against you" from people. This means that the People of the Book, who knew from their scriptures that the Muslim Ummah would face the Ka'bah, would have no valid argument. The only ones who would still argue are "those of them who commit wrong," referring to the polytheists of Quraysh, whose arguments are baseless. The command "So fear them not, but fear Me" directs believers to prioritize the fear of Allah over human criticism. The ultimate purpose of this divine legislation is "that I may complete My favor upon you and that you may be guided," signifying the perfection of the Islamic Shari'ah and the guidance bestowed upon this Ummah, making it the best and most honored nation.

**The Blessings of Prophethood and the Call to Remembrance (2:151-152)**
Ibn Kathir highlights Allah's immense favor upon the believers by sending Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) as a Messenger "from yourselves." This Messenger's mission encompasses several crucial aspects: "reciting to you Our verses" (Ayat), "purifying you" from negative traits and pre-Islamic ignorance, "teaching you the Book" (the Qur'an), "and wisdom" (the Sunnah), and "teaching you that which you did not know." This transformative process elevated the early Muslims from a state of ignorance to one of profound knowledge, piety, and spiritual excellence. In gratitude for these unparalleled blessings, Allah commands the believers in verse 152: "So remember Me; I will remember you. And be grateful to Me and do not deny Me." Ibn Kathir interprets "remember Me" (فَٱذْكُرُونِىٓ) as remembering Allah through obedience to His commands, while "I will remember you" (أَذْكُرْكُمْ) signifies Allah's remembrance of His servant with reward, forgiveness, and mercy. He cites authentic Hadith, such as Allah's statement, "Whoever mentions Me to himself, then I will mention him to Myself; and whoever mentions Me in a gathering, I will mention him in a better gathering," illustrating the profound reciprocal nature of this divine promise. The command "be grateful to Me" (وَٱشْكُرُوا۟ لِى) emphasizes acknowledging and appreciating Allah's countless favors, and "do not deny Me" (وَلَا تَكْفُرُونِ) warns against ingratitude, which entails covering up or failing to acknowledge these blessings. This serves as a foundational principle for maintaining a strong connection with the Divine.
"relevance": "Reinforces Allah's omnipresence and ownership of all directions, providing a theological basis for the Qiblah change."
"relevance": "Clarifies that righteousness is not merely about facing a particular direction, but about faith, good deeds, and piety, contextualizing the Qiblah change within broader Islamic ethics."
"relevance": "Mentions the 'religion of your father Abraham' and Allah's choice of the Muslim Ummah, connecting to the Ka'bah as the Qiblah of Abraham and the Ummah's 'Wasat' status."
"relevance": "Refers to the completion of the religion, which al-Qurtubi links to the Qiblah change as a part of perfecting Allah's blessings."
"point": "Cultivate unwavering obedience to divine commands, recognizing that Allah's wisdom underlies all legislative changes, even if they appear challenging or counter-intuitive."
"point": "Understand the elevated status and responsibility of the Muslim Ummah as a 'middlemost' and just community, tasked with bearing witness to truth and upholding justice."
"point": "Prioritize the fear of Allah (Khashyah) over the fear of human criticism or societal pressure, especially when adhering to religious principles that may be misunderstood or challenged."

"point": "Engage in consistent remembrance (Dhikr) and gratitude (Shukr) to Allah, recognizing His countless blessings, particularly the guidance of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) and the perfection of the Islamic faith."

"point": "Strive to 'race to all that is good' by hastening to perform righteous deeds, especially prayers at their prescribed times, and actively seeking opportunities for spiritual growth and service."

"summary": "These verses address the pivotal change of the Qiblah from Jerusalem to the Ka'bah, explaining its divine wisdom, the reactions of various groups, and its significance for the Muslim Ummah. They emphasize Allah's absolute sovereignty, the test of obedience for believers, and the profound blessings of Prophet Muhammad's mission, culminating in a call for remembrance and gratitude to Allah."
